dialog: The attacks were originally attributed to ghosts and demons.

When the women woke up feeling drowsy and in pain, their bodies bruised and bleeding, many believed they were being made to suffer as punishment for their sins. Many accused the women of lying for attention or to cover up adultery.
Hundreds of times. All of us.
They said we were dreaming. But then we realized that we were dreaming one dream and it wasn’t a dream at all.
They told us that it was Satan. Or the result of wild female imagination.
I’m glad you’re back August. It’s good that you came back.
“Do nothing.”
“Stay and fight.”
"Leave".
"Leave".
I have been in love with Ona Friesen for most of my life. This morning she found me, having lost my faith in everything.
August.
Where are you going?
Nowhere.
Early this morning I saw a squirrel and a rabbit.
Oh.

The squirrel charged the rabbit. Just as the squirrel was about to make contact with the rabbit, the rabbit leapt straight up into the air. Then the squirrel turned around and charged the rabbit from the other direction and the rabbit
leapt into the air and the squirrel missed.
They were playing!
Is that so?
Maybe I wasn’t meant to have seen them playing. It was very early in the morning, and I was the only one roaming around.
But you really saw that?
Yes. I saw it with my own eyes.
We need you.
What do you need me for?
We need you to take the minutes of our meeting.
We must honour our service to each other. We must represent it. Just as the feet of the disciples were washed by Jesus at the Last Supper, knowing that his hour had come.
Stop. You’re tickling me.
God bless you.

At this moment in time, most of the men are gone from the colony. All of the able bodied men, excluding those in wheelchairs, the elderly, and me, the schoolteacher, left yesterday for the city to post bail for the imprisoned attackers, leaving the women free to talk openly with one another.
We will be back in two days.
When they return, in 24 hours, the women will be given the opportunity to forgive these men, guaranteeing everyone’s place in heaven.

My name is August Epp. Two months ago, I returned, from the outside world, to this colony, where I was raised. I am now the colony schoolteacher, and I have been asked to take the minutes of the meetings because the women are functionally illiterate, having had very little education.
Ona Friesen asked me if I would take the minutes, and as I had nothing to do but kill myself, and as I have been in love with her for most of my life and would do anything for her, I agreed.
A vote was held earlier this morning.

As the vote was tied between the option of Staying and Fighting or Leaving, representatives of two families of women, the Reimers and the Friesens, have been tasked with deciding whether or not to Stay and Fight or Leave, while the rest of the women tend to the work of the colony. They have invited representatives of the Janz family to be part of the conversation, though they voted to do nothing. They are meeting in Earnest Penner’s hayloft, as he is senile and rarely comes in.
It is part of our faith to forgive. We have always forgiven those who have wronged us. Why not now?
Because now we know better.
Better than God? You know better than God?
Our Lord requires us to forgive, Salome. Or do you believe yourself mightier than he?
We will be excommunicated, forced to leave the colony in disgrace, if we don’t forgive the men. And if we are excommunicated, we will forfeit our place in heaven.
How could any of you live with the fear of that?
What else are you afraid of Helena? Tell us. We want to hear.
We can only do what we have learned.
Speak up, Helena. We can’t hear you.
We have only domestic skills. How are we supposed to survive out in the World if we are excommunicated?
We are unable to read or write. We’ve never even seen a map.
These are all legitimate fears. How can we address them?
Shouldn’t we be concerned about more than just our survival, Helena? Is what we have lived, worth preserving?
These questions themselves are blasphemous.
Alright. No more blasphemous questions. I want to talk about my horses, Ruth and Cheryl.
Alright. We’ll go.

When Ruth and Cheryl are frightened by Dueck’s Rottweilers on the mile road that leads to the church, their initial instinct is to bolt. These horses don’t organize meetings to decide what they will do. They run.
But Greta, we are not animals.
We have been preyed upon like animals. Maybe we should respond like animals.
Do you mean run away?
Or kill our attackers?
Go. Go to the city. Get the police.
For their own protection. These men need to be taken to jail in the city.
In my lifetime I have seen horses confront angry dogs and try to stomp them to death. Animals don’t always flee their attackers. They can fight back and they can run away.
Either way, it’s a waste of time to try to establish whether we are animals. The men will be coming back from the city after they pay the bail for our attackers. Soon.
The only important thing to establish is whether we forgive the men so that we are allowed to enter the gates of heaven.
Laugh all you want, Salome. But we will be forced to leave the colony if we don’t forgive the men. And how will the Lord, when He arrives, find all the women if we aren’t in our colony?
If Jesus is able to return to life, live for thousands of years and then drop down to earth from heaven, to scoop up his supporters, surely he’d also be able to locate a few women who-
Let’s stay on track-
Alright. I’ll stay on track. I cannot forgive them. I will never forgive them.
I can’t either.
But we want to enter the gates of heaven when we die.
Are we asking ourselves what our priority is? To protect our children or to enter the kingdom of heaven?
No. That is not what we are asking. That is an exaggeration of what we are discussing.
What are we discussing, then?
We will burn that bridge when we come to it.
We have everything we want here.
No.
Want less.
Does entering the kingdom of heaven mean nothing to any of you? After all we have suffered?
Are you really willing to give up what we have always lived for?
Surely there is something in this life worth living for, not only in the next.
Are you abandoning your faith?
We cannot stand by and do nothing when our children are harmed.
How are you protecting your children from harm if you turn your back on God? And how will any of you survive? If you stay and fight you will lose. Or if you leave...
Where will you go?
All I know is that we cannot do nothing. By doing nothing we are not protecting our children who were given to us by God to protect and nurture.
We will not be damned to hell with you.
That is your decision, and we must respect it.
We must decide now whether we will stay and fight or leave. These are the options in front of us. We will not do nothing.
I believe the only solution is to flee.
Is this how we want to teach our daughters to defend themselves? By fleeing?
Not fleeing, but leaving. I am talking about leaving.
I’d rather stand my ground and shoot each man in the heart and bury them in a pit than flee. And I’ll deal with God’s wrath if I have to!
Salome. Aunt Greta is talking about leaving, not fleeing. The word “fleeing” wasn’t what they meant.
Please forgive my mother for using the wrong word. It is a sin so outrageous, that Salome must take it upon herself to rectify for the sake of all humanity.
“Leaving” and “fleeing” are different words. With different meanings. They each say something about us.
August what do you make of all this? Do you have an opinion too?
Well, August?
I think... I think that it is possible to leave something or someone in one frame of mind and arrive elsewhere, in another entirely unexpected frame of mind.
We are already aware of this.
We are aware of many things, in our hearts. But it is good, sometimes, to have them said out loud.
I want to stay and fight.
Won’t we lose the fight to the men and then be forced to forgive them anyway?
Is forgiveness that is forced upon us true forgiveness?
Autje!
Behave yourself. Can’t you hear the rafter creaking? Do you want the roof to cave in?
I want to stay and fight too.

Of course you do. No one is surprised that you do. All you do is fight. Is this how we are to decide the fates of all of the women of this colony? Just another vote where we put an X next to our position? I thought we were here to do more than that.
You mean talk more about forgiving the men and doing nothing?
Everything else is insane. But none of you will listen to reason.
Why are you here then?! Why are you still here with us if that is what you believe?! Leave with the rest of the do-nothing women!
She is my daughter and I want her here with us.
I believe we are capable of hearing opinions other than our own. Or how can we expect anything to change?
This is never going to end.
We’ll be dead and they’ll still be talking.
Or worse. We might have to live through it.
August. I think you should make lists of the pros and cons for both options. Staying and Fighting or Leaving. And write large. Post it on the wall.
Why? We can’t read it.
No. But we will keep it here as an artifact for others to discover.
Yes. A discovery.
I think the first heading should read as follows. Staying and Fighting. Beneath that, write Pros.
Who will go first?

Forgive me. Please excuse me. Forgive me. May I request that you take turns speaking so that I can understand what each of you is saying. It takes me a few seconds to transcribe...I’m a little behind here. I have to catch up.
Shall we put up our hands? As though we are children in your schoolhouse?
I apologize.
We won’t have to leave.
Excuse me?
Write it down. Under pros. Salome has had a brilliant idea.
“If we stay, we won’t have to leave.”
We won’t have to pack.
We won’t have to figure out where we’re going or experience the uncertainty of not knowing where we are going. We don’t have a map.
That’s absurd. The only certainty we’ll know is uncertainty, no matter where we are.
Other than the certainty of the power of love.
Keep nonsense like that to yourself. Please.
Why couldn’t that be the case, that the only certainty is the power of love?
Because it’s meaningless! Especially in this fucking situation!
Stop it. Now. I mean it.
That is disgusting. Truly.
Neitje? Autje? Do you have something to add to the list?
We won’t have to leave the people we love?
We could bring loved ones with us if we leave.
How? What does that mean? We move the whole colony? What can that possibly mean?
Several of the people we love are people we also fear.
We could create the possibility of a new order right here, in a place that is familiar to us.
Not simply familiar. A place that is ours.
Do we need to write the cons? Isn’t it obvious that we must stay and fight?
Cons. We won’t be forgiven.
We don’t know how to fight.
I know how to fight.
We don’t want to fight.
There is the risk that conditions will be worse after fighting than before.
May I speak?
Please.
Would it be a good idea, before we list the pros and cons of staying and fighting, to talk about exactly what we are fighting for?
It’s obvious: we’re fighting for our safety and for our freedom from attacks!

Yes. But what would that mean to us? Perhaps we need a statement which describes what we want the colony to be like after winning the fight. Perhaps we need to know more about what we are fighting to achieve, not only what we are fighting to destroy.
Why don’t we talk about reality instead?
Because our reality is an old one. And we are talking about creating a new reality.
Are we staying or going?
Ona. Please tell us more about the statement you are thinking about.

Men and women will make all decisions for the colony collectively. Women will be allowed to think. Girls will be taught to read and write. The schoolhouse must display a map of the world so that we can begin to understand our place in it. A new religion, taken from the old but focused on love, will be created by the women of the colony.
Our children will be safe.
“Collectively.” You sound like August’s mother.
Ona. You are a dreamer.
We are women without a voice. We have nothing to return to. Even the animals of the colony are safer in their homes than we women are. All we have are our dreams. So of course we are dreamers.
Would you like to hear my dream? I dream that people who speak nonsense, who have no grasp on reality, are not put in charge of making statements!
The statement Ona described sounds good to me. We can add to it over time. For now, it will declare what we women see as the future of the colony, whether we are here or elsewhere. Are we agreed?
What will happen if the men refuse to meet our demands?
We will kill them.
No. Ona. No.
She is only joking.
Maybe not.
What if the men who are in prison are not guilty?
Mother?
Yes I know, Autje.
Then why are you asking-
We caught one of them. I saw him.
Only one.
Yes. Only one. But he named the others.
But what if he was lying?
But the point-
We must consider this.

No! That is not our responsibility! Because we aren’t in charge of whether or not they are punished. We know that we’ve been attacked by men and not by ghosts or Satan as we were led to believe for so long. We know we have not imagined these attacks, that we were made unconscious with cow tranquilizer. We know that we are bruised and infected and pregnant and terrified and insane and some of us are dead. We know that we must protect our children. Regardless of who is guilty!
Alright, Salome, thank you, please sit down.
Shall we move on?
But if there is any chance that the men in prison are innocent, shouldn’t we be joining forces to secure their freedom?
It is possible that the men in prison are not guilty of the attacks. But are they guilty of not stopping the attacks? Are they guilty of knowing about the attacks and doing nothing?
How should we know what they’re guilty of or not?

But we do know. We do know that the conditions have been created by men and that these attacks have been made possible because of the circumstances of the colony. And those circumstances have been created and ordained by the men.

But wait, aren’t you suggesting that the attackers are as much victims as the victims of the attacks? That all of us, men and women, are victims of the circumstances from which the colony has been created?
In a sense, yes.
So then, even if the court finds them guilty or innocent, they are, after all, innocent?
Yes, I would say so.
The elders called them evil.
But that’s not true.
It’s the elder’s quest for power that is responsible.
Yes because they needed to have those-
Those they’d have power over.
And those people are us.
And they have taught this lesson of power to the boys and men of the colony and the boys and men have been excellent students.
But don’t we all want some type of power?
Yes, I think so. But I’m not sure.
The only thing we can be sure of is that time is disappearing.
But... we caught them. We caught them.
Yes.
Yes you did.
Then why are you making it so complicated?
It’s very, very boring.
We could ask the men to leave.
Is that a joke?
Are you crazy, Ona?
No, no...
Ask the men to leave?!

None of us have ever asked the men for anything. Not a single thing, not even for the salt to be passed, not even for a penny or a moment alone or to take the washing in or to open a curtain or to go easy on the small yearlings or to put your hand on the small of my back as I try, again, for the twelfth or thirteenth time, to push a baby out of my body. Isn’t it interesting, that the one and only request we women would have of the men would be to leave?
It’s not an option. They wouldn’t leave.
Asking the men to leave is not an option. I’d like you to imagine Ruth and Cheryl-
Oh no, not again.
Please stop. I’m afraid I’ll go into labour!
Look! August is still taking the minutes!
You must think we’re all lunatics.
I don’t. And it doesn’t matter what I think, anyway.
Do you think that’s true? That it doesn’t matter what you think?
How would you feel if in your entire lifetime it had never mattered what you thought?
But I’m not here to think. I’m here to take the minutes of your meeting.
But if, in all your life, you truly felt that it didn’t matter what you thought, how would that make you feel?
When we have liberated ourselves, we will have to ask ourselves who we are.
Will we be done by suppertime? I have to give little Miep her antibiotics.
Where did you get antibiotics?
She walked. She walked for a day and a half to the mobile klinic. With Miep on her back.
The Lord is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, rich in loving kindness and forgiveness.
I have to hide the antibiotics in Miep’s apple sauce or she won’t swallow them.
The Lord is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, rich in loving kindness and forgiveness.
The Lord is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, rich in loving kindness and forgiveness.
I’m not crying. I’m moisturizing.
Work, for the night is coming, Work, through the morning hours; Work, while the dew is sparkling, Work ‘mid springing flowers;
Forgive me. They are too big for my mouth.
Well. Let’s take a break.
Don’t say a word about my smoking. Honestly.
Is she always going to be like this now?
Like what?
Like a man. Is Nettie always going to be a man now?
I think she always felt she wasn’t a woman. What happened to her just made it...final.
Is my brother listening?
Is my brother listening?
He is.

Hello, little brother. I don’t know if it was your baby or one of your friends. But I think it was likely yours. Because there was something wrong with it. Small as a bun, but with everything intact. I loved it, I think. Isn’t that strange? I won’t speak of it... or anything else. Ever again.

Hello, little brother. I don’t know if it was your baby or one of your friends. But I think it was likely yours. Because there was something wrong with it. Small as a bun, but with everything intact. I loved it, I think. Isn’t that strange? I won’t speak of it... or anything else. Ever again.
But…
But what?
She doesn’t speak anymore.
She speaks to the children. I think they call her Melvin.
I mean it. Not one word about my smoking.

You know, during the second world war, in Italy, civilians would hide in bomb shelters. Volunteers were needed to power the generators that provided electricity. They rode bikes. When you were swinging from the rafter earlier, it reminded me of this. You would have been the perfect volunteer. If we were in a bomb shelter.
Where would I ride the bike to in such a small space?
Ah yes. Well, the bike would be stationary.
I have to get the water to the yearlings.
Watch this.
I’ll bet you didn’t learn how to do things like that when you went to University.
Only facts about stationary bicycles in far off places.
I suppose I shouldn’t be too sad then. That I won’t ever go.
Why were you forced to leave?
My mother questioned things.
She questioned God?
Not God. Power. The rules that are made in the name of God. She encouraged others to question things too.
Like Aunt Ona?
Yes. Ona knew her well.
Did she die?
But sometimes, listening to all of you speaking today, I can hear her so clearly.
Why did the elders let you come back?
I went to university. So I could serve a purpose and teach the boys.
Too late.
I want to help. And I don’t know how.
You came back for Aunt Ona didn’t you? The way you look at her is... funny. I don’t know why she won’t just marry you. You both say so much that doesn’t make sense.
I am here to collect data for the 2010 census.
I am here to collect data for the 2010 census. All residents must come out of their homes to be counted.
Where is Autje?
Well. We must begin without her.
Were you smoking?
Is that any of your business?
Please.
We must decide this afternoon about staying or leaving.
I can’t live a second longer! Life is too cruel!
Autje! Wait until I get ahold of you!
I could have had a heart attack!
Excuse me. Excuse me. The Census taker just told us that one of our men is planning to return late tonight. He is coming to get some old horses to auction.
Ruth and Cheryl!
They need more bail money for the attackers.
Tonight?
Which man?
Father.
Your Klaas.
Oh.
So. Time is of the essence. Everyone get back to your seats.
Are you trying to call us to order?
No. No. Please forgive me.
Why are you here? Why is my presence questioned when there is a man-
Pros for leaving:
We will be gone?
We will be safe.
Perhaps not. But the first is most definitely a fact, that if we leave we will be gone.
Do we really have time to state the obvious over and over?
Add to the list this: We will not be asked to forgive the men, because we will not be here to hear the question.
Yes. Autje?
We will see a bit of the world?
Let’s move on to the Cons of Leaving.

We, the women, will decide what happens in these meetings. Not a two-bit failed farmer who must teach. You have been invited here. You have been invited here to listen to what we have to say and to write it down. Nothing more. Just. Listen.

Mariche! Klaas is returning soon and you are wasting time! Klaas will return to your home for just long enough to take his animals in order to sell for bail money that will see the rapists return to the colony and he will lay his hands on you and on your children, and you, as always, do nothing but fire away at us all like a Gatling gun with your misdirected rage. What good does that do?
I would like to apologize for wrongly attempting to nudge the proceedings. That is not my place.
Mejal!
I'll stop.
We don’t have a map. We don’t know where to go.
We don’t even know where we are!
Hush. Put your hair away.
I hurt.
There are no Cons of Leaving.
Not to worry, Nettie.
If we do leave the colony, how will we live with the pain of not seeing our brothers and our sons again? The men?
Time will heal. Our freedom and safety are the ultimate goals, and it is men who prevent us from achieving those goals.
But not all men.
Perhaps not men, but a way of seeing the world, and us women, that has been allowed to take hold of men’s hearts and minds.
So if we leave... if we leave... I will never see my brothers again?
Who will take care of them? Of them all?
We can’t know if we will stay or leave before we resolve these last- minute concerns.
I wouldn’t call the future of our relationships with the boys and men we love “last-minute concerns.”
I need some water.
It’s good to have you with us, August. To remind us of what is possible. Because it’s easy to forget.
I am so sorry, Ona.
One day, I would like to hear those words from someone who should be saying them.
Why does love... the absence of love, the end of love, the need for love, result in so much violence?
Ona.
Ona. I could take care of you and your child. I want to. I-
I know August. You don’t have to say the words.
If I were married I would not be myself. And so the person you love would be gone.
Your child-
If we stay and don’t win the fight, my child will be given to another family here. Maybe even to the family of my attacker. If we stay and we don’t win the fight.
You won’t let that happen.
No. No I won’t.
August. We want to discuss options for the men and the older boys, if the women decide to leave.
Which is a waste of time because we are not leaving.
The men should be allowed to leave with the women if they wish.
Then what on earth is the point of us leaving?
They could be allowed to join the women later, when the women have established themselves and are thriving.
Should we add, thriving as a collective, literate community?
Literate is your word. Not ours. We don’t need your university language to make our plans.
Put it in. We know what it means. Continue.
Young boys, simple minded boys of any age, Cornelius who is confined to a wheelchair, will accompany the women.
I vote for the first option. They should leave with us if they wish.
The first option is ridiculous and should be crossed off the list.
Why are some ideas written down and considered, and others crossed out?
I want to leave.
I’d like to strangle myself.
But Mariche, it’s possible that all the men would choose to leave with us. Then all we’d be doing is re- creating our colony, with all of its dangers elsewhere, wherever we end up.
And the men would most definitely leave with us because they can’t survive without us.
Well, not for longer than a day or two.
There is no possibility of the men leaving with us. Whatever we decide. And we have not decided to leave. I would like to remind everyone of that.
Clearly these are unrealistic ideas. And how are we to leave at all if we have never been allowed to even see a map of the world?
I can secure a world map for you.
Where on earth would you get a map August?
I also have a map of this specific region.
That will do. We aren’t planning to travel the planet.
Perhaps we are. Did you know that the migration period of butterflies and dragonflies is so long that it is often only the grandchildren who arrive at the intended destination?
So. Yes, so.
Perhaps, if we went beyond where the map shows us, we could create our own map as we go.
Now that is a unique idea.
So now you want to leave? Ona?
Oh.
I’m fine.
She’s having one of her episodes.
Please, God. Restore Mejal’s equilibrium.
Almighty Father, in all humility and supplication we ask Thee for Thy abundant kindness this moment.
Please, in your beneficence, heal her. Please, we ask of Thee, envelop her in your strength and everlasting love, and please drive out the sickness that afflicts her now.
Take the cigarette out of her pocket.
Alright. Help me up.
Praise be to God.
Why is it only Mejal who has these sudden-
Be quiet.
We were all attacked. Not all of us draw so much attention to ourselves.
Attention? What attention? I talk less than all of you put together. How have I offended you?
You have these “attacks.” You smoke. Why? Why is it so much harder for you than for us? We were all attacked. All of us. And the rest of us are all able to get through a day without-

We are wasting time by passing this burden, this sack of stones, from one to the next, by pushing our pain away. We mustn’t do this. We mustn’t play hot potato with our pain. Let’s absorb it ourselves, each of us. Let’s inhale it, let’s digest it, let’s process it into fuel.
Speak, Mejal. We are listening.
They made us...they made us disbelieve ourselves. That was worse than...
Mejal?
Perhaps Mejal’s episode was brought on by the thought of us creating our own map.
But I will draw it if we need-
Not a fear of the do-it-yourself map making. But of what it means: that we are masters of our own destiny. That we would be setting off into unknowable space.
Yes. It makes sense that one would panic.
I am not panicking.
Yes. But panic, in this case would be understandable.
But I’m not.
Klaas, when he returns, may take horses or livestock that we will need along the way.
Along the way? We’re not leaving. You are changing your mind, Ona.
I don’t believe that is a sin, is it?

How will we be forgiven for all this? How will we be forgiven if not by the elders whom we have disobeyed and who, if we leave, we will never see again. It will leave us unforgiven, with black hearts, and unable to enter the kingdom of god.
Perhaps there will be other elders or men of God that will be able to forgive us our sins. Ones we haven’t met yet.

We do not have to be forgiven by the men of God for protecting our children from the depraved actions of vicious men who are often the very same men we are meant to ask for forgiveness! If God, in the book of Matthew asks: Let the children come to me and do not hinder them, then mustn’t we consider it a hindrance when our children are attacked? If God is a loving God He will forgive us Himself. If God is a vengeful God then He has created us in His image. If God is omnipotent then why has He not protected the women and girls of this colony? I will destroy any living thing that harms my child. I will tear it limb from limb, I will desecrate its body and I will bury it alive. I will challenge God on the spot to strike me dead if I have sinned by protecting my child from evil and by destroying the evil that it may not harm another! I will lie, I will hunt I will kill and I will dance on graves and burn forever in hell before I allow another man to satisfy his violent urges with the body of my four-year-old child!
No. Not dancing. Not desecration.
Salome.

I suggest that we think of what is good. “Whatever is true, whatever is honourable, whatever is just, whatever is pure, whatever is pleasing, whatever is commendable, if there is any excellence and if there is anything worthy of praise, think about these things... and the peace of God be with you.”

Whatever is true, whatever is honourable, whatever is just, whatever is pure, whatever is pleasing, whatever is commendable, if there is anything worthy of praise, think about these things… and the peace of God be with you.
I will become a murderer if I stay. What is worse than that?
I know what these are. These are letters. But what are these?
They are commas. They signify a short pause, or a breath, in the text.
There is also a butterfly called the comma.
Is that so?
Yes. It’s called the comma because-
No. Let me guess. Because it flits about from leaves to petals, only briefly stopping on its way? Because its journey is its story, never stopping, only pausing, only moving.
Aha!

Salome, there is nothing worse than being a murderer. If you will become a murderer by staying in the colony, side by side with the men who are responsible for the attacks then you must, to protect your own soul and to qualify for entry to heaven, leave the colony.
We are not all murderers.
Not yet.

I have done what the verse from Philippians instructed, which is to think about what is good, what is just, what is pure, and what is excellent. And I have arrived at an answer. Pacifism. Pacifism is good. Any violence is unjustifiable. By staying here, we women would be betraying the central tenet of our faith, which is pacifism, because by staying we would knowingly be placing ourselves in a direct collision course with violence, either by us or against us.
This colony is the only home I’ve ever known, and I don’t want to leave. But by staying, we would be inviting harm. We would be in a state of war. We would turn this colony into a battlefield.
We cannot become murderers. And we cannot endure any more violence. That is why we must leave.
Let’s shake a leg, then.

I’m also thinking about the verse from Philippians and I’m thinking about what is good. Freedom is good. It’s better than slavery. And forgiveness is good. Better than revenge. And hope for the unknown is good, better than hatred of the familiar.
What about security and safety and home and family? What about marriage and love?

I don’t know about those things, any of them. Except for love. And even love is mysterious to me. And I believe that my home is with my mother, with my sister and with my unborn child, wherever they may be.
Will you not hate that child? That child is the child of a man who inspires violent thoughts in you.
Mother! Again!
I already love this child more than anything.
He or she is as innocent and lovable as the evening sun. And so too was the child’s father when he was born.
Are you crying?
If you are saying that forgiveness is better than revenge, aren’t you saying that we must stay here and forgive the men?

We cannot forgive because we are forced to. But if there is distance, perhaps I can begin to understand how these crimes may have occurred. And maybe from that distance, I can pity these men, and perhaps forgive them. And even love them.
And so we must leave in order to have that distance.
Not fighting. But moving on. Always moving. Never fighting. Just moving...
Snap out of it.
You snap out of it, Mariche.
All of you snap out of it and focus. Have you lost your minds? The sun is gone.
I want to tell another story about Ruth and Cheryl.

I was always frightened of the northern road out of the colony. So many gullies on either side of the road that are so deep. And it’s so narrow. The buggy used to lurch side to side. Ruth and Cheryl were simply following my commands on the reins but they were jerky and frenetic. It was dangerous. It was only when I learned to focus my gaze far down ahead of me, down the road, and not on the road immediately in front of Ruth and Cheryl that I started to feel safe.
Leaving will give us the more far- seeing perspective we need to forgive.
Which is to love properly, and to keep the peace, according to our faith.

Therefore, our leaving wouldn’t be an act of cowardice or abandonment. It wouldn’t be because we were excommunicated or exiled. It would be a supreme act of faith, a step towards love and forgiveness.
Leaving is how we demonstrate our faith. We are leaving because our faith is stronger than the rules. Bigger than our life.
I am sorry. But I think I might be dying.
Mother. You are not dying. Your glasses need cleaning.
I thought the lights were going out.
What are you laughing at? We can’t leave. It would be better to stay and fight than leave.

Do you really mean that you want to stay and NOT fight? Because when was the last time you had the strength to stand up to the aggression of Klaas, to protect your children, or to get out of harm’s way?
Who are you to tell me what kind of wife and mother to be when you are neither one yourself? You are a spinster, a lunatic! A whore! An unwed mother!

Ona was made unconscious and raped like the rest of us and now is pregnant as a result! How dare you call her a whore! Mariche, are you not afraid your own sweet boys will become monsters like their father because you do nothing to protect them or yourself-
Stop.
Nothing to educate them, nothing to teach them the horror of their father’s ways, the sickness...

Now. I. Have. Heard. Enough! Are you women not aware that we are talking about leaving? We are a large group. Many things can go wrong and our time is fleeting! For the love of our Lord Jesus Christ and precious Saviour will you shut your pieholes, please!
How dare you pass judgement on me.
It wasn’t judgement. It was a question.
I am sorry, Mariche. I am sorry that what I said hurt you.
Fuck it off.
Sit down Mariche.
It’s “fuck off” I think.
I am sorry. I am saying sorry, not just to leave the hurt behind, but because I feel, truly, that I should not have said something harmful.
And Mariche. I am sorry because you don’t need or deserve more harm.
Who are any of you to pretend I have had a choice?
I am also sorry Mariche.

Because, Mariche. I couldn’t - I didn’t try to protect you or your children from Klaas. All this time. And what you say is true. You had no choice. You forgave him, again and again, as you were told to. As I told you to.
It is not only the men and boys who have been excellent students.
Yes. All of us have been infected by a poisoned way of thinking.
And so you have judged me. For what I have endured.
I think, Mariche... I think that we are all very sorry. What you have been required to endure with your violent husband was a...
A misuse of forgiveness.
Is there such a thing? Is there a forgiveness that is not good?
Perhaps forgiveness can, in some instances, be confused with permission.
Perhaps it will also be a difficult task to forgive each other, and ourselves, after all that has happened.
What in heaven’s name?
Nettie. Please. Be reasonable. Make an exception and tell us what is happening. There are only women in this loft. Nettie!
What has happened to him?
Nettie. Be realistic. What has happened to Julius? Please! Just speak this once!
My nose. There is a cherry pit in my nose!
Blow. Now. Blow, Julius.
If Julius has put a cherry pit up his nose it means he has been eating cherries or he has, at least, been near cherries.
We have no cherries in the colony.
Klaas sometimes brings them back from the city.
Who gave you the cherries? Julius!
Papa.
Papa is home now?
No. I saw him out on the road. He’s collecting animals.
If you see him again you can tell him that we are all quilting.
Isn’t that lying?
No. It’s... something else. Go now. Go with Nettie.
Have we made a decision? Are we leaving?
Yes.
We are leaving because…
We know why we are leaving. We are leaving because we cannot stay.
What happens when we become hungry? Or afraid?
We are not animals. Hunger and fear cannot be our guide.
Should we not have more perspective than animals?
Animals have perspective. Remember? The dragonflies? They set out knowing that they will not see the end of their journey but their children will.
Please for the love of Joshua Judges Ruth can we start talking practically!
I like that. “For the love of Joshua Judges Ruth.”
We will take young boys under twelve with us. And we will allow the men to join the women later, under certain conditions.
I like it.
Me too.
Would everyone agree to this now, knowing that our minds may change in the future?
No. Not yet.
We can’t leave.
Aaron. I know.
He is just over twelve. Just barely.
The sadness of leaving Aaron behind for the time being will only spur us all, all of us grieving mothers, to rebuild a new and better colony for everyone.
Why are boys aged thirteen and fourteen left behind? Why wouldn’t they leave with us?
Surely we can’t be afraid of boys of this age? Why couldn’t they join us if we leave?
August. You’re the boys teacher. What is your feeling about this? Do boys of this age pose a threat to our girls and women?
Yes. Possibly. Every one of us, male or female, poses a potential threat. Thirteen and fourteen-year- old boys are capable of causing great damage to girls and women, and to each other.

It is a brash age. They are possessed of reckless urges, physical exuberance, intense curiosity that often results in injury, unbridled emotion, including deep tenderness and empathy, and not quite enough experience or brain development to fully understand or appreciate the consequences of their actions or words. They are similar to the yearlings; young, awkward, gleeful, powerful. They are tall, muscular, sexually inquisitive creatures with little impulse control, but they are children. They are children and they can be taught. I’m a two-bit schoolteacher, a failed farmer, an effeminate man, and above all, a believer.

I believe that with direction, firm love and patience these boys are capable of relearning their roles as males in the colony. I believe in what the great poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge thought were the cardinal rules of early education. “To work by love and so generate love. To habituate the mind to intellectual accuracy and truth. To excite imaginative power.” He said “Little is taught by contest or dispute, everything by sympathy and love.”
I believe those boys should be allowed to leave with the women, providing the women choose to leave.
It was a yes or no question.You shit like any other man, why don’t you talk like one?
Sorry.
I won’t leave Aaron.
I would like to make a proposal. We must protect all of our children. Not only our daughters. All boys under the age of fifteen, and the ones that require special care must accompany the women.
How do you mean must?
What if they refuse? If they don’t want to leave?
We can’t carry teenagers on our backs.
We will try to influence our sons. But we cannot force them, and they may refuse, it is true.
But that would be very sad.
Let’s talk about our sadness after we have nailed down our plan. August, you would stay here to teach the boys who remain?
What’s the point in trying to teach them? Fifteen-year-old boys still believe that throwing horse turds at the girls while we do the milking shows their love.
But a boy who truly loves you will intentionally miss when he throws the shit, or not throw it with quite as much force.
My most hopeful dream for my four year old girl is that one happy day a boy will intentionally miss hitting her with a clump of shit.
Yes. The day every mother dreams of, the hope that gets us through the darkest hours.
He’s here.
Klaas.
He has Ruth and Cheryl!
Everyone, back to your houses. Go collect your children, and pack up. August, get the map. Greta and I will pack the food supplies. If anyone asks, we were quilting here.
Neitje and Autje! You must run now to every house and tell the women, we are leaving!
Tell them to bring everything they can. We will assemble outside the washhouse!
And pin up your hair!
I am worried about the women who have voted to do nothing. If Klaas, or any other man has returned, there is a high risk that these women will inform them that we are plotting.
We must have faith that the Do Nothing women will not inform on us.
But some, like Scarface Janz, believe that to fight or to leave is a sin. What about her?
What about her, August?
Do you have faith in her?
I must have faith in all of us, right now.
Mariche. Be careful.
Breathe, mother.
Breathe and slow down. You always hold your breath when you’re exerting yourself.
Don’t laugh while you’re on the ladder. Concentrate.
We will have to get an early start tomorrow morning. Let’s meet here again at sunrise. All of us.
Don’t go. Stay with me tonight. Or I will go with you.
If I don’t go home, it will draw attention to all of us. I must behave as though everything isn’t about to change.
Mariche.
Go home, Mother. I will see you at sunrise.
An hour after sunrise. We congregate on the road by the wash house.
Do we bring-
Everything. Bring everything.
Why?
We meet an hour after sunrise. On this road. We need your buggy.
Thank you, sister.
An hour after sunrise we leave. We meet behind the wash house.
And we need your buggies. Both of them.
Tomorrow?
You will be there?
I have so much to do.
So. We will need your help. The horses need to be brushed. Saddled.
Mama. I’m hurting.
The pills aren’t working. I think they are for calves, not people.
But she is small. They’ll work.
She is small. But she’s not a calf.
Psst! August!
Come. Sit with me.
Here is the map.
Where are we?
Here.
Here. This is where we are.
I’ve created a legend.
For the map. I’ve drawn asterisks on the map that coincide with pictures in the legend.
What do the pictures show?
Rivers, roads, towns and cities and borders, train tracks. See?
This is north... south... east... and west.
But the map moves. How do we know which direction the map should be facing?
Celestial navigation. Let me show you.
Do you know of the Southern Cross?
You... and the women, can use the Southern Cross for navigation. If you clench your right fist like this-
Now align your first knuckle with the axis of the Cross.
Now. The tip of your thumb, here, will indicate south.
Will you show the others?
We will have a lesson in navigation.
Ona.
Did you already know about this little trick?
Of course. Of course I did.
I wish there was something I could tell you that you didn’t already know.
What will become of you when we leave?
I hope... I hope that you can help the boys. I hope that you can help them to be truthful. And to listen. Like you do.
Where are Mariche and Autje?
They will be here. I pray they will be here.
Concentrate on lifting your feet higher when you take steps. Don’t shuffle. You’ll trip again.
I am very tired. My body is heavy.
Please. Please. Be gentle. You are blinding me.
August. Did you dream last night?
Yes.
Is he gone?
He’s sleeping. Dead to the world. He was very drunk.
Tell me what happened.
Father caught me sneaking back into the house, late and then he went to the barn and found we had taken the horses.
Did you tell him what we were planning?
She did. But it was because he wouldn’t stop hitting me and she was trying to distract him.
Yes. But I also told him because I suddenly felt very...
I don’t think he believed me. And if he did, I don’t think he’ll remember. He’ll be passed out in the barn all morning I’m sure.
So. Yesterday was a day for talking. Today is a day for action. When Klaas wakes up he may go to the city to alert the other men. We have decided to leave before that happens. Is that accurate?
We have ruled out the option of staying because-
I thought today was a day of action, not talk.
We have decided that we want... that we are entitled to three things.
What are they?
We want our children to be safe.
We want to be steadfast in our faith.
We want to think.
Yes.
Praise God.
Yes, that’s it.
Precisely.
Well it’s not precisely put. But it sounds perfect to me. A perfect beginning.
Salome, will you use your last breath on earth to correct me?
Yes, if that is what is needed.
What if we feel guilt? What if it overwhelms us?
We will feel pain and we will feel uncertainty and we will feel sadness, but not guilt.
We may feel guilty but we will know we are not guilty.
We may feel homicidal, but we will know we are not killers.
We may feel vengeful, but we will know we are not raccoons.
We may feel lost, but we will know we are not losers.
Speak for yourself.
I always do. You should try it too.

We may feel guilt and we may feel sadness. But we will endure it. We’re embarking on a journey. We are making a change that we have interpreted as being a testament to our faith and to our instincts as mothers. We must believe in it.
We don’t know everything that will happen. But we’ve made our plan. And, yes, we must believe in it.
You can speak whenever you want, August, and you don’t have to raise your hand. You’re the teacher!
It’s alright. It wasn’t important.
There is work to do. We must stop talking and prepare to leave.
Uncle Penner!
Earnest!
What are you doing here in my loft? Are you angels? Are you lost? Will you help me with my bath?
What are you bitches plotting?
Oh, Earnest. My sweet cousin. We’re getting old, aren’t we?
Are you devils?
No. We’re your friends.
Are you plotting to burn down my barn?
No, Ernie. There’s no plot. We’re only women talking.
Will you help me with my bath?
Why don’t I take you back to your house and give you a washing. I’ll give you a bath and get you something to eat.
Will you make sure the water you use to wash Earnest is warm, but not hot, not scalding?
Be quick.
There is mint growing next to the lower barn door! You could pick some of it and add it to the warm water. Earnest would love that!
Mother?
I’m just saying goodbye.
I’m nervous.
We’re all nervous. We can’t avoid nervousness.
We hid Ruth and Cheryl for you. They are ready to go.
Ruth and Cheryl!!! Really??!!!
Well, my girls.
We’ll head out, then.
Yes. Let’s go.
We’ll need to find guns, in case anyone tries to stop us.
No. We won’t have guns. We must begin peacefully. As we mean to continue.
We don’t know where we are going.
We don’t. We can’t. But we must go anyway.
Are the children clean and ready?
And their things are packed? They are fed?
Thank you, Melvin.
Thank you. Thank you for saying my name.
Melvin, are you ready for the journey?
No. I am not ready.
But I am coming with you.
Yes, who of us can say we’re ready, after all?
I can.
Melvin, please return to the children and play a game with them in the field next to the wash house. That is where the other women will find us, on their way out of the colony.
Has Aaron readied the horses for us?
No.
What? Where is he?
Speak to me. Please. I won’t harm you. I am not your enemy!
You must calm down, Salome. Aaron will be found. Melvin. You are safe.
But we’re leaving soon. I’m not leaving without him.
Salome! Come back!
Salome! Aaron will be found. He will leave with us. I know he will!
But what if she doesn’t convince Aaron?
Mother?
We are going to go now.
Now?
Yes. Make a list, August.
A list of what?
Of good things. Of memories, of plans. Whatever you feel goes into a good list; what we, the women, would want there, please write it down.
Thank you, August. For all you have done. We are all so proud of you. Your mother would be too.
I will make a list.
This will be a difficult trip.
I’m aware of that. Today is the day that the Lord hath made. Let us rejoice and be glad in it!
I won’t be buried in this colony. Help me into a buggy now and I’ll die on the trail.
What about August?
August, wouldn’t you marry my Ona?
I’ve asked her so many times.
And she always said no?
Hang on with both hands.
What about Aaron?
Ona. I will always love you!
And she loves you too, August. She loves everyone.

How will I live without these women? My heart will stop. I will try to teach the boys and men about these women, about the new reality of which they dream. I must make a list. A list, from the Middle English liste, meaning desire. Which is also the origin of the word “listen.”
Sun.
Stars.
Pails.
Birth.
The Harvest.
Numbers.
Sounds. Window.
Straw. Beams.
Love.
I will do what God commands me to do. I will not lie for you.
Futility.
Language.
Wind.
Women.
We may need to protect ourselves.
Where is Aaron?
He is in the buggy, waiting.
You convinced him to leave?
Here. You may need this. For protection.
Did you have to-
Yes. It’s just as though I had picked up a sleeping child in the night and carried him away from a house that was on fire.
Is it?
He’s coming with me. He’s my child.
I broke the rules? I did. I broke the new rules already. Maybe I’ve broken everything. And we haven’t yet begun. I sprayed Scarface Janz also. She was planning to go to the city to tell the men.
Does she know how to get there?
No, of course not.
So it was an idle threat.
But I was afraid.
Klaas too. But he wasn’t awake yet, so he won’t remember being knocked out. Just like we didn’t.
Goodbye August, and good luck.
Please take care of Ona and her baby.
Of course. I promise.
Wait. I need to give you something.
Here.
Why do you have a gun, August?
Because...
Don’t kill yourself August. You have important work to do.
You are the boys teacher.
We really have to hurry.
But you’re not fleeing.
That’s right. We’ve chosen to leave.
But not Aaron.
I will have to live with that.
Don’t come back. Don’t ever come back, any of you.
I’ll miss you. Be a good teacher. You have straw in your hair.
Wait! I have to give you the minutes!
August! I have to go!
Please give these to Ona.
But she can’t read them.
Her child will read them.
August. The purpose was for you to take the minutes.
We’ll meet again.
We’ll meet again.
